sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A metered dose inhaler with spacer is an effective way to deliver inhaled bronchodilator therapy in many prehospital patients. It can be quicker, cleaner, and less aerosol-generating than nebulisation, provided the patient can cooperate and move enough air to use it effectively.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Prepare the inhaler and spacer according to device instructions
2. Shake and insert the inhaler correctly
3. Coach the patient to seal around the mouthpiece or use a mask if appropriate
4. Deliver one actuation at a time and allow the patient to inhale through the spacer before repeating
5. Reassess symptom relief, work of breathing, and objective observations after treatment

sections.title: Clinical Considerations
sections.content:
- Spacer therapy is often appropriate for mild to moderate bronchospasm when the patient can cooperate
- If the patient is severely breathless, exhausted, poorly cooperative, or unable to use the device effectively, escalate to other delivery methods according to local protocol
- Infection-control and spacer hygiene still matter, especially in shared or stocked devices
- The chosen delivery method should be based on patient capability and severity, not habit alone

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use one actuation at a time rather than stacking multiple puffs into the chamber
- Document the drug, number of actuations, and response
- MDI with spacer can be highly effective when technique is good, but reassessment is essential to confirm that the patient is improving
